name: AP Biology
description: AP Biology is a rigorous college-level course covering the core principles of modern biology, including the chemistry of life, cellular processes, genetics, evolution, and ecology. Students develop scientific reasoning skills through inquiry-based learning and prepare for the AP Biology exam.

modules.title: Chemistry of Life
modules.objectives: Understand the chemical composition of living organisms, including the properties of water, carbon-based molecules, and the four macromolecules: carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Biology 2e, Chapter 2: The Chemical Foundation of Life — focus on sections 2.1-2.4 covering atomic structure, chemical bonds, water properties, and carbon chemistry.
modules.videoNote: CrashCourse Biology #1: Carbon — explains why carbon is the backbone of biological molecules and introduces organic chemistry concepts.
modules.video2Note: CrashCourse Biology #3: Water — the molecule that makes life possible, covering hydrogen bonds, cohesion, adhesion, and pH.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy AP Biology: Chemistry of Life — complete the practice exercises on macromolecules and water properties.
modules.assignment: Create a concept map linking the four macromolecules (carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, nucleic acids) with their monomers, functions, and examples found in the human body. Submit as a digital or hand-drawn diagram.

modules.title: Cell Structure and Function
modules.objectives: Compare and contrast prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells. Identify and describe the function of major cell organelles including the nucleus, mitochondria, endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi apparatus, chloroplasts, and cytoskeleton.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Biology 2e, Chapter 4: Cell Structure — read sections 4.1-4.4 on microscopy, prokaryotic cells, eukaryotic cells, and organelle functions.
modules.videoNote: CrashCourse Biology #4: Eukaryopolis — an entertaining tour through eukaryotic cell organelles and their functions.
modules.video2Note: Amoeba Sisters: Prokaryotic vs. Eukaryotic Cells — clear comparison of the two cell types with diagrams.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy: Cell Structure — complete the organelle identification exercises and the cell membrane quiz.
modules.assignment: Draw and label a detailed diagram of an animal cell and a plant cell. For each of 8 organelles, write a 1-sentence analogy comparing the organelle to a part of a city or school. Explain why plant cells have chloroplasts and cell walls while animal cells do not.

modules.title: Membrane Transport
modules.objectives: Explain the fluid mosaic model of the cell membrane. Distinguish between passive transport (diffusion, osmosis, facilitated diffusion) and active transport. Describe endocytosis and exocytosis.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Biology 2e, Chapter 5: Structure and Function of Plasma Membranes — sections 5.1-5.4 covering membrane structure, passive transport, active transport, and bulk transport.
modules.videoNote: CrashCourse Biology #5: In Da Club — Membranes and Transport — covers the phospholipid bilayer, membrane proteins, and all forms of membrane transport.
modules.video2Note: Khan Academy: Osmosis — detailed explanation of osmosis with hypertonic, hypotonic, and isotonic solution examples.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy: Membrane Transport — work through the diffusion, osmosis, and active transport practice problems.
modules.assignment: Design an experiment to test osmosis in potato cells. Include hypothesis, materials, procedure, expected results for hypotonic/isotonic/hypertonic solutions, and explain what would happen at the cellular level in each solution type.

modules.title: Cell Communication
modules.objectives: Describe the three stages of cell signaling: reception, transduction, and response. Explain how ligands bind to receptors and how signal transduction cascades amplify signals. Compare local and long-distance signaling.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Biology 2e, Chapter 9: Cell Communication — read sections 9.1-9.4 on signaling molecules, cell-surface receptors, intracellular signal transduction, and cellular response.
modules.videoNote: CrashCourse Biology #44: Cell Membranes and Signaling — covers receptor types and signal transduction pathways with real-world examples.
modules.video2Note: Bozeman Science: Cell Communication — AP Biology review covering the three stages of signaling with clear diagrams.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy: Cell Communication — complete the signal transduction pathway exercises and the receptor types quiz.
modules.assignment: Choose one real-world example of cell signaling (e.g., insulin signaling or the fight-or-flight response). Write a 300-word explanation tracing the signal from receptor binding through transduction to cellular response, using correct biological terminology.

modules.title: Cell Cycle and Division
modules.objectives: Describe the stages of the cell cycle including interphase (G1, S, G2) and mitosis (prophase, metaphase, anaphase, telophase). Explain how checkpoints regulate the cell cycle and how dysregulation leads to cancer.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Biology 2e, Chapter 10: Cell Reproduction — read sections 10.1-10.3 on the cell cycle, mitosis, and cancer as a failure of cell cycle control.
modules.videoNote: CrashCourse Biology #10: Mitosis — explains cell division with clear animations covering all phases of mitosis and cytokinesis.
modules.video2Note: Amoeba Sisters: Mitosis vs. Meiosis — visual comparison that clarifies the purpose and process of mitotic cell division.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy: Cell Cycle — practice identifying cell cycle stages from microscope images and answer checkpoint regulation questions.
modules.assignment: Using a microscope slide image set of onion root tip cells, identify and count cells in each phase of mitosis. Calculate the percentage of time spent in each phase and explain what this tells us about the relative duration of each phase. Relate abnormal cell division to cancer development.

modules.title: Mendelian Genetics
modules.objectives: Apply Mendel's laws of segregation and independent assortment. Solve monohybrid and dihybrid cross problems using Punnett squares. Distinguish between dominant, recessive, codominant, and incompletely dominant traits.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Biology 2e, Chapter 12: Mendel's Experiments and Heredity — read sections 12.1-12.3 on Mendel's laws, probability in genetics, and extensions to Mendelian genetics.
modules.videoNote: CrashCourse Biology #9: Heredity — Mendel's pea plant experiments and the laws of inheritance explained clearly.
modules.video2Note: Bozeman Science: Mendelian Genetics — AP Biology-focused review of Punnett squares, genotype vs. phenotype, and complex inheritance patterns.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy: Mendelian Genetics — work through the Punnett square practice problems and the dihybrid cross exercises.
modules.assignment: Solve a genetics problem set: (1) monohybrid cross with incomplete dominance, (2) dihybrid cross with independent assortment, (3) chi-square analysis to test if observed offspring ratios match expected ratios. Show all work and explain your reasoning.

modules.title: DNA Structure and Replication
modules.objectives: Describe the double helix structure of DNA and the roles of nucleotides, base pairing, and antiparallel strands. Explain the process of DNA replication including the roles of helicase, primase, DNA polymerase, and ligase.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Biology 2e, Chapter 14: DNA Structure and Replication — read sections 14.1-14.5 covering the history of DNA discovery, double helix structure, and semi-conservative replication.
modules.videoNote: CrashCourse Biology: DNA Structure and Replication — clear explanation of the Watson-Crick model and the replication process.
modules.video2Note: HHMI BioInteractive: DNA Replication animation showing the replication fork and enzyme activities in real time.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy: DNA Replication — complete the leading strand/lagging strand practice questions and enzyme function matching exercises.
modules.assignment: Draw a detailed diagram of the replication fork showing all major enzymes (helicase, primase, DNA polymerase III, DNA polymerase I, ligase) and label: leading strand, lagging strand, Okazaki fragments, RNA primer. Write a paragraph explaining why DNA replication is semi-conservative and why this matters for genetic fidelity.

modules.title: Gene Expression: Transcription and Translation
modules.objectives: Explain the central dogma of molecular biology. Describe transcription (DNA to mRNA) and translation (mRNA to protein) including the roles of RNA polymerase, ribosomes, tRNA, codons, and anticodons.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Biology 2e, Chapter 15: Genes and Proteins — read sections 15.1-15.5 on the genetic code, transcription, RNA processing, and translation.
modules.videoNote: CrashCourse Biology #11: DNA, Hot Pockets, and The Longest Word Ever — covers transcription, translation, and the genetic code.
modules.video2Note: HHMI BioInteractive: From DNA to Protein — high-quality animation of transcription and translation with molecular detail.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy: Transcription and Translation — complete the codon table practice and the mRNA-to-protein translation exercises.
modules.assignment: Given the DNA template strand 3'-TACGGGCTTACCCGT-5', write the corresponding mRNA sequence, identify the codons, use a codon table to determine the amino acid sequence, and explain the effect of a point mutation at the 5th nucleotide (missense, nonsense, or silent?).

modules.title: Biotechnology
modules.objectives: Describe key biotechnology tools including PCR, gel electrophoresis, restriction enzymes, CRISPR-Cas9, and recombinant DNA technology. Explain applications in medicine, agriculture, and forensics. Discuss ethical considerations.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Biology 2e, Chapter 17: Biotechnology and Genomics — read sections 17.1-17.3 on cloning, biotechnology applications, and genomics.
modules.videoNote: CrashCourse Biology #20: Biotechnology — covers recombinant DNA, PCR, gel electrophoresis, and GMOs with real-world applications.
modules.video2Note: Kurzgesagt: CRISPR and Genetic Engineering — engaging explanation of CRISPR-Cas9 technology, how it works, and its potential applications.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy: Biotechnology — complete the PCR process steps quiz and the gel electrophoresis interpretation exercises.
modules.assignment: Design a biotechnology experiment using PCR and gel electrophoresis to determine if a person carries a specific genetic variant. Write a step-by-step protocol, draw expected gel results for homozygous dominant, heterozygous, and homozygous recessive individuals, and discuss the ethical implications of genetic testing.

modules.title: Natural Selection and Evolution
modules.objectives: Explain Darwin's theory of evolution by natural selection including variation, heritability, differential reproduction, and adaptation. Describe Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium and the factors that cause allele frequency changes.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Biology 2e, Chapters 18 and 19: Evolution and the Evolution of Populations — read sections on natural selection, evidence for evolution, and population genetics.
modules.videoNote: CrashCourse Biology #14: Natural Selection — explains the four conditions for natural selection with classic examples like the peppered moth.
modules.video2Note: Bozeman Science: Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium — AP Biology review showing how to calculate allele and genotype frequencies and apply the H-W equation.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy: Natural Selection — complete the Hardy-Weinberg calculations and the types of natural selection practice problems.
modules.assignment: Using the Hardy-Weinberg equation, analyze a population where 9% of individuals show a recessive phenotype. Calculate allele frequencies (p and q), genotype frequencies, and determine if the population is in H-W equilibrium given new data. Identify which evolutionary force might be acting and justify your answer.

modules.title: Speciation and Phylogeny
modules.objectives: Distinguish between allopatric, sympatric, and parapatric speciation. Explain reproductive isolating mechanisms. Interpret phylogenetic trees (cladograms) and understand how they represent evolutionary relationships.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Biology 2e, Chapters 18 and 20: Phylogenies and the History of Life — read sections on speciation mechanisms, reproductive isolation, and cladistics.
modules.videoNote: CrashCourse Biology #15: Speciation — covers geographic and reproductive isolation with the concept of species and how new species form.
modules.video2Note: Bozeman Science: Phylogenetics — how to read and construct phylogenetic trees, parsimony, and the use of molecular data in systematics.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy: Phylogeny — complete the cladogram interpretation exercises and the speciation mechanisms quiz.
modules.assignment: Analyze a provided phylogenetic tree of 6 species and answer: (1) which two species share the most recent common ancestor, (2) identify a synapomorphy for a given clade, (3) propose whether allopatric or sympatric speciation is more likely for two species pairs. Justify all answers.

modules.title: Ecology: Populations
modules.objectives: Define population ecology and key parameters: population size, density, dispersion, birth rate, death rate, and growth rate. Compare exponential and logistic population growth. Explain carrying capacity and limiting factors.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Biology 2e, Chapter 45: Population and Community Ecology — read sections 45.1-45.3 on population demographics, population growth, and regulation.
modules.videoNote: CrashCourse Ecology #3: Population Ecology — covers exponential vs. logistic growth, carrying capacity, and r vs. K selection strategies.
modules.video2Note: Bozeman Science: Population Ecology — AP Biology review with mathematical models for population growth and age structure diagrams.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy: Population Ecology — complete the logistic growth model calculations and the survivorship curve interpretation problems.
modules.assignment: Analyze population data for a fictional deer population over 20 years. Plot the data, identify whether the population shows exponential or logistic growth, estimate the carrying capacity, identify two limiting factors, and predict what would happen if a new predator were introduced.

modules.title: Community Ecology
modules.objectives: Describe interspecific interactions including competition, predation, mutualism, commensalism, and parasitism. Explain community succession (primary and secondary). Describe species diversity and its importance for ecosystem stability.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Biology 2e, Chapter 45: Population and Community Ecology — read sections 45.6-45.8 on species interactions, ecological succession, and biodiversity.
modules.videoNote: CrashCourse Ecology #4: Community Ecology — explains all types of interspecific interactions with memorable examples and their ecological significance.
modules.video2Note: Amoeba Sisters: Ecological Relationships — visual overview of symbiosis, competition, and predator-prey dynamics with real ecosystem examples.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy: Community Ecology — complete the interspecific interaction classification exercises and the ecological succession timeline problems.
modules.assignment: Create an interaction web for a local ecosystem (forest, grassland, or marine) with at least 8 species, label each interaction type (competition, predation, mutualism, etc.), identify keystone species, and write a 250-word essay predicting how the removal of the keystone species would affect community structure.

modules.title: Ecosystems and Energy Flow
modules.objectives: Explain energy flow through ecosystems using trophic levels, food webs, and ecological pyramids. Describe the carbon and nitrogen biogeochemical cycles. Discuss how human activities disrupt ecosystem processes and contribute to climate change.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Biology 2e, Chapter 46: Ecosystems — read sections 46.1-46.4 on ecosystem energy dynamics, food webs, biogeochemical cycles, and ecosystem disruption.
modules.videoNote: CrashCourse Ecology #5: Ecosystems — energy flow, ecological efficiency, and the 10% rule for energy transfer between trophic levels.
modules.video2Note: Bozeman Science: Biogeochemical Cycles — detailed review of the carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus, and water cycles with human impact connections.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy: Ecosystems and Energy — complete the ecological pyramid calculations and the biogeochemical cycle matching exercises.
modules.assignment: Using the 10% rule of energy transfer, calculate how much plant matter is needed to support a 150-pound human eating only beef (tertiary consumer) vs. chicken (secondary consumer) vs. plants (primary consumer). Discuss the environmental implications of diet choices and relate them to carbon cycle disruption.

modules.title: Ecosystems and Energy Flow — Advanced Applications
modules.titleZh: 生态系统与能量流动 — Advanced Applications
modules.objectives: In-depth case studies and real-world applications.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Biology 2e, Chapter 46: Ecosystems — read sections 46.1-46.4 on ecosystem energy dynamics, food webs, biogeochemical cycles, and ecosystem disruption. (Advanced)
modules.videoNote: CrashCourse Ecology #5: Ecosystems — energy flow, ecological efficiency, and the 10% rule for energy transfer between trophic levels. (Review)
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy: Ecosystems and Energy — complete the ecological pyramid calculations and the biogeochemical cycle matching exercises. (AP Prep)
modules.assignment: Complete an in-depth analysis or practice AP-style free response questions on this topic.

modules.title: Ecosystems and Energy Flow — Synthesis & Review
modules.titleZh: 生态系统与能量流动 — Synthesis & Review
modules.objectives: Comprehensive review and AP exam preparation strategies.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Biology 2e, Chapter 46: Ecosystems — read sections 46.1-46.4 on ecosystem energy dynamics, food webs, biogeochemical cycles, and ecosystem disruption. (Advanced)
modules.videoNote: CrashCourse Ecology #5: Ecosystems — energy flow, ecological efficiency, and the 10% rule for energy transfer between trophic levels. (Review)
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy: Ecosystems and Energy — complete the ecological pyramid calculations and the biogeochemical cycle matching exercises. (AP Prep)
modules.assignment: Complete an in-depth analysis or practice AP-style free response questions on this topic.

quizQuestions.question: Which property of water allows it to act as a solvent for ionic compounds?
options: Its polar covalent bonds and hydrogen bonding
quizQuestions.answer: Its polar covalent bonds and hydrogen bonding
quizQuestions.explanation: Water is a polar molecule with partial positive and negative charges. This polarity allows water to surround and dissolve ionic compounds by forming hydration shells around ions.

quizQuestions.question: Which macromolecule serves as the primary long-term energy storage in animals?
quizQuestions.explanation: Lipids (specifically triglycerides/fats) are the primary long-term energy storage molecules in animals. They store more than twice the energy per gram compared to carbohydrates.

quizQuestions.question: The monomers of proteins are:
quizQuestions.explanation: Proteins are polymers made up of amino acid monomers linked by peptide bonds. There are 20 standard amino acids that combine in different sequences to form all proteins.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which property of water allows it to act as a solvent for ionic compounds" is: Its polar covalent bonds and hydrogen bonding.
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Its polar covalent bonds and hydrogen bonding is correct.

quizQuestions.question: Which organelle is responsible for synthesizing proteins?
quizQuestions.explanation: Ribosomes are the site of protein synthesis (translation). They can be found free in the cytoplasm or attached to the rough endoplasmic reticulum.

quizQuestions.question: What is the primary function of the Golgi apparatus?
quizQuestions.explanation: The Golgi apparatus acts like the cell's post office -- it receives proteins from the ER, modifies them (e.g., glycosylation), and packages them into vesicles for delivery to their final destinations.

quizQuestions.question: Which structure is found in plant cells but NOT animal cells?
quizQuestions.explanation: Plant cells have cell walls (made of cellulose) and chloroplasts for photosynthesis, neither of which are present in animal cells. Both cell types have mitochondria, ribosomes, and a nucleus.

quizQuestions.question: Prokaryotic cells differ from eukaryotic cells in that prokaryotes:
quizQuestions.explanation: The defining difference between prokaryotes and eukaryotes is that prokaryotes lack membrane-bound organelles (including a nucleus). Their DNA is in a region called the nucleoid region.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which organelle is responsible for synthesizing proteins" is: Ribosomes.
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Ribosomes is correct.

quizQuestions.question: In osmosis, water moves from an area of:
options: High solute concentration to low solute concentration
Low solute concentration to high solute concentration
High pressure to low pressure only
quizQuestions.answer: Low solute concentration to high solute concentration
quizQuestions.explanation: Osmosis is the diffusion of water across a semipermeable membrane. Water moves from hypotonic (low solute) to hypertonic (high solute) solutions, effectively moving toward where there is more solute.

quizQuestions.question: Which type of transport requires ATP?
quizQuestions.explanation: Active transport requires energy (ATP) to move substances against their concentration gradient (from low to high concentration). The sodium-potassium pump is a classic example of active transport.

quizQuestions.question: A red blood cell placed in a hypertonic solution will:
quizQuestions.explanation: In a hypertonic solution (higher solute concentration outside), water leaves the cell by osmosis, causing the red blood cell to shrink and become crenated (shriveled).

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "In osmosis, water moves from an area of:" is: Low solute concentration to high solute concentration.
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Low solute concentration to high solute concentration is correct.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which type of transport requires ATP" is: Simple diffusion.
quizQuestions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Active transport.

quizQuestions.question: Which stage of cell signaling involves a ligand binding to a receptor?
quizQuestions.explanation: Cell signaling has three stages: reception (ligand binds to receptor), transduction (signal is converted and amplified through a cascade), and response (cell activity changes).

quizQuestions.question: G protein-coupled receptors activate signal transduction by:
options: Activating G proteins that stimulate secondary messengers
Forming ion channels upon ligand binding
quizQuestions.answer: Activating G proteins that stimulate secondary messengers
quizQuestions.explanation: When a ligand binds to a GPCR, it activates a G protein (GDP to GTP). The activated G protein then stimulates enzymes that produce second messengers like cAMP, which amplify the signal inside the cell.

quizQuestions.question: Which molecule often acts as a second messenger in signal transduction?
quizQuestions.explanation: Cyclic AMP (cAMP) is a common second messenger. It is produced from ATP by adenylyl cyclase and amplifies signals inside the cell by activating protein kinases like protein kinase A (PKA).

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is an example of long-distance cell signaling in animals?
options: Gap junctions between adjacent heart cells
Direct cell-to-cell contact between immune cells
quizQuestions.explanation: Endocrine signaling (hormones) is long-distance signaling where molecules are released into the bloodstream and travel to distant target cells. Neurotransmitters act locally at synapses.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which stage of cell signaling involves a ligand binding to a receptor" is: Reception.
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Reception is correct.

quizQuestions.question: During which phase of the cell cycle does DNA replication occur?
quizQuestions.explanation: DNA synthesis (replication) occurs during the S (synthesis) phase of interphase. After S phase, the cell has doubled its DNA content in preparation for cell division.

quizQuestions.question: During metaphase of mitosis, chromosomes:
options: Align at the cell's equatorial plate
Separate and move to opposite poles
quizQuestions.answer: Align at the cell's equatorial plate
quizQuestions.explanation: During metaphase, chromosomes are maximally condensed and align at the metaphase plate (cell's equator), attached to spindle fibers at their centromeres.

quizQuestions.question: The G1/S checkpoint primarily checks for:
options: Correct chromosome attachment to spindle fibers
Adequate cell size and absence of DNA damage
quizQuestions.answer: Adequate cell size and absence of DNA damage
quizQuestions.explanation: The G1/S checkpoint ensures the cell is large enough, has adequate nutrients, and has no DNA damage before committing to DNA replication and cell division.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "During which phase of the cell cycle does DNA replication occur" is: S phase.
quizQuestions.explanation: True. S phase is correct.

quizQuestions.question: Fill in the blank: Complete: In the context of the topic, ___ refers to "how many chromosomes does a human somatic cell have after completing mitosis."
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: 46.

quizQuestions.question: In a monohybrid cross between two heterozygous individuals (Aa x Aa), what is the expected phenotype ratio?
quizQuestions.explanation: A cross of Aa x Aa produces offspring in genotype ratio 1 AA : 2 Aa : 1 aa. If A is dominant, 3/4 show the dominant phenotype and 1/4 show the recessive phenotype, giving a 3:1 phenotype ratio.

quizQuestions.question: Mendel's Law of Independent Assortment applies when genes are:
options: Located on different chromosomes or far apart on the same chromosome
quizQuestions.answer: Located on different chromosomes or far apart on the same chromosome
quizQuestions.explanation: Independent assortment applies to genes on different chromosomes or very far apart on the same chromosome. Linked genes (close together on the same chromosome) tend to be inherited together.

quizQuestions.question: In incomplete dominance, a cross between red (RR) and white (WW) flowers produces:
quizQuestions.explanation: In incomplete dominance, the heterozygote shows an intermediate phenotype. RR x WW produces all RW offspring with pink flowers because neither allele is completely dominant over the other.

quizQuestions.question: A test cross involves crossing an unknown dominant phenotype individual with:
quizQuestions.explanation: A test cross crosses an individual of unknown genotype (but dominant phenotype) with a homozygous recessive individual (aa). The ratio of offspring phenotypes reveals the unknown parent's genotype.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following best describes codominance?
options: One allele masks the expression of another
Both alleles are fully expressed simultaneously in the heterozygote
The heterozygote shows an intermediate phenotype
quizQuestions.answer: Both alleles are fully expressed simultaneously in the heterozygote
quizQuestions.explanation: In codominance, both alleles in a heterozygote are fully and simultaneously expressed. Type AB blood is a classic example -- both A and B antigens are present on red blood cells.

quizQuestions.question: DNA strands are described as antiparallel because:
options: One strand runs 5' to 3' and the complementary strand runs 3' to 5'
They are made of different sugars
quizQuestions.answer: One strand runs 5' to 3' and the complementary strand runs 3' to 5'
quizQuestions.explanation: The two strands of DNA run antiparallel -- one from 5' to 3' and the complementary strand from 3' to 5'. This antiparallel orientation is essential for base pairing and DNA replication.

quizQuestions.question: Which enzyme unwinds the double helix during DNA replication?
quizQuestions.explanation: Helicase unwinds and separates the two DNA strands at the replication fork by breaking the hydrogen bonds between complementary bases, creating single-stranded templates for DNA polymerase.

quizQuestions.question: DNA replication is described as semi-conservative because:
options: Only half of the DNA is replicated each cycle
Each new DNA molecule has one original and one new strand
The process conserves exactly half the energy
DNA polymerase only works on half the chromosome
quizQuestions.answer: Each new DNA molecule has one original and one new strand
quizQuestions.explanation: Semi-conservative replication means each new double helix contains one original (parental) strand and one newly synthesized strand. This was confirmed by the Meselson-Stahl experiment using nitrogen isotopes.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "DNA strands are described as antiparallel because:" is: One strand runs 5' to 3' and the complementary strand runs 3' to 5'.
quizQuestions.explanation: True. One strand runs 5' to 3' and the complementary strand runs 3' to 5' is correct.

quizQuestions.question: Which enzyme synthesizes mRNA from a DNA template?
quizQuestions.explanation: RNA polymerase transcribes DNA into mRNA during transcription. It reads the DNA template strand in the 3' to 5' direction and synthesizes mRNA in the 5' to 3' direction.

quizQuestions.question: The central dogma of molecular biology describes information flow as:
quizQuestions.explanation: The central dogma (Crick, 1958) states that genetic information flows from DNA (transcription) to RNA (translation) to Protein. While reverse transcription exists in retroviruses, the general flow is DNA to RNA to Protein.

quizQuestions.question: Which type of RNA carries amino acids to the ribosome during translation?
quizQuestions.explanation: tRNA is the adapter molecule in translation. Each tRNA has an anticodon that pairs with an mRNA codon and carries the corresponding amino acid to the ribosome, where it is added to the growing polypeptide chain.

quizQuestions.question: A codon is best defined as:
options: A three-nucleotide sequence in DNA that codes for a protein
A three-nucleotide sequence in mRNA that specifies an amino acid
The entire sequence of a gene
quizQuestions.answer: A three-nucleotide sequence in mRNA that specifies an amino acid
quizQuestions.explanation: A codon is a three-nucleotide sequence on mRNA that codes for a specific amino acid (or start/stop signal). The genetic code has 64 codons specifying 20 amino acids.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which enzyme synthesizes mRNA from a DNA template" is: RNA polymerase.
quizQuestions.explanation: True. RNA polymerase is correct.

quizQuestions.question: PCR (Polymerase Chain Reaction) amplifies DNA by:
options: Cycling through denaturation, annealing, and extension steps
Using RNA polymerase to copy DNA
quizQuestions.answer: Cycling through denaturation, annealing, and extension steps
quizQuestions.explanation: PCR amplifies specific DNA sequences through repeated temperature cycles: denaturation (~95 C separates strands), annealing (~55-65 C primers bind), and extension (~72 C Taq polymerase synthesizes new DNA).

quizQuestions.question: In gel electrophoresis, smaller DNA fragments:
options: Travel less distance than larger fragments
Travel the same distance regardless of size
Travel farther from the well than larger fragments
quizQuestions.answer: Travel farther from the well than larger fragments
quizQuestions.explanation: In gel electrophoresis, DNA (negatively charged) migrates toward the positive electrode. Smaller fragments move more easily through the gel matrix and travel farther, while larger fragments stay closer to the well.

quizQuestions.question: CRISPR-Cas9 functions as a genome editing tool because:
options: It methylates specific DNA sequences to silence genes
The guide RNA directs Cas9 to a specific DNA sequence where it makes a precise cut
It inserts random mutations throughout the genome
It copies genes from one organism to another without cutting
quizQuestions.answer: The guide RNA directs Cas9 to a specific DNA sequence where it makes a precise cut
quizQuestions.explanation: CRISPR-Cas9 uses a guide RNA (gRNA) complementary to the target DNA sequence to direct the Cas9 enzyme precisely to that location. Cas9 then cuts both strands of DNA.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "PCR (Polymerase Chain Reaction) amplifies DNA by:" is: Cycling through denaturation, annealing, and extension steps.
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Cycling through denaturation, annealing, and extension steps is correct.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "In gel electrophoresis, smaller DNA fragments:" is: Travel less distance than larger fragments.
quizQuestions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Travel farther from the well than larger fragments.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is NOT a requirement for natural selection to occur?
options: Conscious choice by organisms to adapt
quizQuestions.answer: Conscious choice by organisms to adapt
quizQuestions.explanation: Natural selection requires: variation in heritable traits, differential reproduction (some variants survive/reproduce better), and heritability. Evolution is not directed or intentional.

quizQuestions.question: The Hardy-Weinberg equation p2 + 2pq + q2 = 1 represents:
options: The rate of mutation in a population
Expected genotype frequencies in a non-evolving population
The relationship between population size and genetic diversity
The rate of speciation in isolated populations
quizQuestions.answer: Expected genotype frequencies in a non-evolving population
quizQuestions.explanation: The Hardy-Weinberg equation predicts genotype frequencies in a population NOT experiencing evolution. p2 = frequency of homozygous dominant, 2pq = heterozygous, q2 = homozygous recessive.

quizQuestions.question: Genetic drift has the greatest effect on:
quizQuestions.explanation: Genetic drift (random changes in allele frequencies due to chance) has the greatest impact on small populations where chance events can cause large changes in allele frequencies.

quizQuestions.question: Directional selection is occurring when:
options: Both extreme phenotypes are favored over the intermediate
One extreme phenotype is favored over others
quizQuestions.answer: One extreme phenotype is favored over others
quizQuestions.explanation: Directional selection favors one extreme phenotype, shifting the population mean in one direction over time. Antibiotic resistance -- bacteria with the highest resistance are most fit and proliferate.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which of the following is NOT a requirement for natural selection to occur" is: Conscious choice by organisms to adapt.
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Conscious choice by organisms to adapt is correct.

quizQuestions.question: Allopatric speciation occurs when:
options: Two populations develop reproductive isolation while living in the same geographic area
A geographic barrier separates a population, preventing gene flow
Polyploidy creates a new species in a single generation
Reproductive isolation develops due to different mating seasons
quizQuestions.answer: A geographic barrier separates a population, preventing gene flow
quizQuestions.explanation: Allopatric speciation occurs when a physical barrier (mountain range, ocean, river) divides a population. Without gene flow, the two populations evolve independently until they become reproductively isolated.

quizQuestions.question: A synapomorphy is:
options: A trait shared by all organisms
A derived character shared by all members of a clade
An ancestral trait found only in outgroups
A trait that evolved multiple times independently
quizQuestions.answer: A derived character shared by all members of a clade
quizQuestions.explanation: A synapomorphy is a derived (evolved, not ancestral) character shared by all members of a particular clade (monophyletic group). Synapomorphies define clades and are used to construct phylogenetic trees.

quizQuestions.question: Prezygotic reproductive isolating mechanisms include:
options: Behavioral isolation, habitat isolation, and temporal isolation
quizQuestions.answer: Behavioral isolation, habitat isolation, and temporal isolation
quizQuestions.explanation: Prezygotic isolating mechanisms prevent mating or fertilization: habitat isolation, temporal isolation, behavioral isolation, and mechanical isolation. Postzygotic mechanisms act after fertilization.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Allopatric speciation occurs when:" is: A geographic barrier separates a population, preventing gene flow.
quizQuestions.explanation: True. A geographic barrier separates a population, preventing gene flow is correct.

quizQuestions.question: A population's carrying capacity (K) is best defined as:
options: The maximum growth rate of the population
The maximum population size the environment can sustainably support
The number of organisms born per year
The population size at which birth rate equals death rate only due to resource competition
quizQuestions.answer: The maximum population size the environment can sustainably support
quizQuestions.explanation: Carrying capacity (K) is the maximum population size that the environment's resources can sustainably support. When a population reaches K, growth rate approaches zero in the logistic growth model.

quizQuestions.question: Exponential population growth is characterized by:
options: Growth that slows as the population approaches carrying capacity
A constant growth rate regardless of population size
Growth that accelerates as resources become limiting
Population size oscillating around the carrying capacity
quizQuestions.answer: A constant growth rate regardless of population size
quizQuestions.explanation: Exponential growth occurs when resources are unlimited and each individual reproduces at a constant rate (r). The population grows at an ever-increasing rate producing a J-shaped curve.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is a density-dependent limiting factor?
options: Disease spreading through a crowded population
quizQuestions.answer: Disease spreading through a crowded population
quizQuestions.explanation: Density-dependent factors have greater effects as population density increases. Disease, predation, and competition for resources are density-dependent. Natural disasters are density-independent.

quizQuestions.question: A Type III survivorship curve describes a species where:
options: Most individuals survive to old age
Survival rate is constant throughout life
Most individuals die young with few surviving to reproductive age
quizQuestions.answer: Most individuals die young with few surviving to reproductive age
quizQuestions.explanation: Type III survivorship curves (common in oysters, plants, insects) show high early mortality with few individuals surviving to adulthood. These species typically have many offspring with little parental care.

quizQuestions.question: r-selected species typically show which combination of traits?
options: Few offspring, long lifespan, high parental care
Many small offspring, rapid maturation, short lifespan, low parental care
Stable populations at carrying capacity, large body size
Slow reproduction, high offspring survival rate
quizQuestions.answer: Many small offspring, rapid maturation, short lifespan, low parental care
quizQuestions.explanation: r-selected species maximize reproductive rate: many small offspring, rapid maturation, short lifespan, little parental care. They thrive in unpredictable environments.

quizQuestions.question: The competitive exclusion principle states that:
options: Predators always eliminate their prey over time
Two species with identical niches cannot coexist in the same habitat
Competition between species always leads to symbiosis
quizQuestions.answer: Two species with identical niches cannot coexist in the same habitat
quizQuestions.explanation: Gause's competitive exclusion principle states that two species competing for identical resources cannot stably coexist -- one will outcompete and exclude the other.

quizQuestions.question: A keystone species is best described as a species that:
options: Has the largest population in the community
Has a disproportionately large effect on community structure relative to its abundance
Is at the top of the food chain
Is the most recently evolved in the community
quizQuestions.answer: Has a disproportionately large effect on community structure relative to its abundance
quizQuestions.explanation: A keystone species has an ecological impact far greater than expected from its numbers or biomass. Removing a keystone species causes dramatic changes in community structure and diversity.

quizQuestions.question: Primary succession differs from secondary succession in that primary succession:
options: Occurs faster because soil is already present
Begins on barren substrate with no soil or biological legacy
Always leads to a different climax community
quizQuestions.answer: Begins on barren substrate with no soil or biological legacy
quizQuestions.explanation: Primary succession starts where no soil or biological community existed (bare rock, volcanic lava fields). It begins with pioneer species that create soil. Secondary succession occurs where soil remains.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "The competitive exclusion principle states that:" is: Two species with identical niches cannot coexist in the same habitat.
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Two species with identical niches cannot coexist in the same habitat is correct.

quizQuestions.question: The 10% rule in ecology states that:
options: Only 10% of species survive an extinction event
Only 10% of energy is transferred from one trophic level to the next
Populations grow at 10% per year under ideal conditions
10% of plant matter is consumed by herbivores
quizQuestions.answer: Only 10% of energy is transferred from one trophic level to the next
quizQuestions.explanation: On average, only about 10% of energy stored in organisms at one trophic level is available to the next level. The rest (90%) is lost as metabolic heat, respiration, or unusable biomass.

quizQuestions.question: In the carbon cycle, which process removes carbon dioxide from the atmosphere?
quizQuestions.explanation: Photosynthesis removes CO2 from the atmosphere as plants and other autotrophs convert it into organic compounds using light energy. Respiration, combustion, and decomposition all release CO2 back into the atmosphere.

quizQuestions.question: Nitrogen fixation is the process of:
options: Plants absorbing nitrate ions from soil
Converting atmospheric N2 into ammonia (NH3) by bacteria
Denitrifying bacteria releasing N2 from nitrates
Animals excreting urea into the environment
quizQuestions.answer: Converting atmospheric N2 into ammonia (NH3) by bacteria
quizQuestions.explanation: Nitrogen fixation converts atmospheric nitrogen gas (N2, which most organisms cannot use) into ammonia (NH3) or ammonium (NH4+). This is performed by nitrogen-fixing bacteria like Rhizobium in legume root nodules.

quizQuestions.question: Which trophic level contains the most energy in an ecosystem?
quizQuestions.explanation: Primary producers (plants, algae, cyanobacteria) capture solar energy through photosynthesis and are the foundation of the food web. They contain the most total energy.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "The 10% rule in ecology states that:" is: Only 10% of energy is transferred from one trophic level to the next.
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Only 10% of energy is transferred from one trophic level to the next is correct.

questions.question: Which property of carbon allows it to form the diverse molecules found in living organisms?
options: Carbon can form 4 covalent bonds and bond to itself to form chains and rings
Carbon is the most abundant element in the universe
Carbon only bonds with oxygen and hydrogen
questions.answer: Carbon can form 4 covalent bonds and bond to itself to form chains and rings
questions.explanation: Carbon's tetravalency allows it to form 4 covalent bonds. It can bond to other carbons creating chains, branched structures, and rings, providing the structural diversity of organic molecules.

questions.question: An enzyme is a protein that functions as a:
options: Biological catalyst that lowers activation energy
questions.answer: Biological catalyst that lowers activation energy
questions.explanation: Enzymes are biological catalysts -- they speed up chemical reactions by lowering the activation energy required. They bind substrates at their active site and are not consumed in the reaction.

questions.question: The fluid mosaic model of the cell membrane describes:
options: A rigid bilayer of proteins with lipids embedded in it
A dynamic phospholipid bilayer with embedded and peripheral proteins
A static structure with fixed channels
A single layer of phospholipids with proteins on the outside
questions.answer: A dynamic phospholipid bilayer with embedded and peripheral proteins
questions.explanation: The fluid mosaic model (Singer and Nicolson, 1972) describes the membrane as a fluid phospholipid bilayer with various proteins (integral and peripheral) that can move laterally.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "When a cell is placed in a hypotonic solution, water will move:" is: Into the cell by osmosis, causing the cell to swell.
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. Into the cell by osmosis, causing the cell to swell is correct.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the stages of mitosis in order?
questions.answer: Prophase, Metaphase, Anaphase, Telophase
questions.explanation: Mitosis proceeds: Prophase (chromosomes condense, spindle forms), Metaphase (chromosomes align at equator), Anaphase (sister chromatids separate to poles), Telophase (nuclear envelopes reform). PMAT is a common mnemonic.

questions.question: In pea plants, tall (T) is dominant over short (t). What fraction of offspring from a cross of Tt x tt will be tall?
questions.explanation: Tt x tt produces: 1/2 Tt (tall) and 1/2 tt (short). Since T is dominant, Tt plants are tall. Therefore 1/2 (50%) of offspring will be tall.

questions.question: Which nitrogenous base is found in DNA but NOT in RNA?
questions.answer: Thymine
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Thymine.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which of the following best describes the role of the ribosome in gene expression" is: It transcribes DNA into mRNA.
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: It serves as the site where mRNA is translated into protein.

questions.question: A signal transduction pathway amplifies a signal because:
options: Each step in the cascade activates multiple molecules of the next messenger
The signal molecule grows larger as it moves through the cell
questions.answer: Each step in the cascade activates multiple molecules of the next messenger
questions.explanation: In a signal transduction cascade, each activated protein activates many copies of the next protein, creating an exponential amplification effect where a few ligand-receptor events can activate thousands of effector molecules.

questions.question: PCR requires a heat-stable DNA polymerase because:
options: The denaturation step requires temperatures (~95 C) that would destroy regular DNA polymerase
questions.answer: The denaturation step requires temperatures (~95 C) that would destroy regular DNA polymerase
questions.explanation: The denaturation step in PCR requires heating to ~95 C to separate DNA strands. Taq polymerase (from Thermus aquaticus, a thermophilic bacterium) is heat-stable and remains functional at these temperatures.

questions.question: The endoplasmic reticulum (ER) can be categorized as rough or smooth based on:
options: The presence or absence of ribosomes on its surface
Whether it synthesizes lipids or proteins
questions.answer: The presence or absence of ribosomes on its surface
questions.explanation: Rough ER is studded with ribosomes (giving it a rough appearance) and is involved in protein synthesis and modification. Smooth ER lacks ribosomes and is involved in lipid synthesis, detoxification, and calcium storage.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Helicase's role in DNA replication is to:" is: Unwind the double helix at the replication fork.
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. Unwind the double helix at the replication fork is correct.

questions.question: In mRNA, the start codon is:
questions.answer: AUG
questions.explanation: AUG (methionine codon) is the universal start codon in mRNA. It signals where translation begins and encodes the first amino acid (methionine) in all proteins. UAA, UAG, and UGA are stop codons.

questions.question: Which of the following is a membrane-bound organelle found only in plant cells?
questions.answer: Chloroplast
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Chloroplast.

questions.question: The lac operon in E. coli is an example of:
options: Eukaryotic gene regulation by transcription factors
Prokaryotic gene regulation where genes are expressed only when lactose is present and glucose is absent
questions.answer: Prokaryotic gene regulation where genes are expressed only when lactose is present and glucose is absent
questions.explanation: The lac operon's genes for lactose metabolism are expressed when lactose is present (repressor inactive) and glucose is absent (CAP activator active). Both conditions must be met for full expression.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "A population of beetles has 840 individuals. If p (frequency of allele A) = 0.3, what is the expected frequency of heterozygous individuals (2pq) under Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium" is: 0.09.
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: 0.42.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes stabilizing selection?
options: Favors one extreme phenotype in the population
Favors both extreme phenotypes and eliminates intermediate phenotypes
Favors intermediate phenotypes and eliminates extremes
Has no effect on population phenotype distribution
questions.answer: Favors intermediate phenotypes and eliminates extremes
questions.explanation: Stabilizing selection favors intermediate phenotypes, reducing variation while maintaining the current population mean. Human birth weight is a classic example.

questions.question: Two bird species live in the same forest but one eats insects from the upper canopy and the other eats insects from the lower shrubs. This is an example of:
questions.answer: Resource partitioning
questions.explanation: Resource partitioning occurs when competing species use resources differently, reducing direct competition and allowing coexistence by occupying slightly different niches.

questions.question: Using the 10% energy transfer rule, if producers contain 10,000 kcal of energy, how much energy is available to secondary consumers?
questions.answer: 100 kcal
questions.explanation: Primary producers (10,000 kcal) give 10% to Primary consumers = 1,000 kcal. Secondary consumers get 10% of 1,000 = 100 kcal. Each transfer loses approximately 90% of energy.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which of the following correctly describes allopatric speciation" is: Speciation due to geographic separation that prevents gene flow.
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. Speciation due to geographic separation that prevents gene flow is correct.

questions.question: The process of nitrogen fixation is important in the nitrogen cycle because:
options: It converts nitrates into atmospheric N2
It makes atmospheric nitrogen available to plants in a usable form
It decomposes organic matter into ammonia
It converts ammonia to nitrites and nitrates
questions.answer: It makes atmospheric nitrogen available to plants in a usable form
questions.explanation: Plants cannot use atmospheric N2 directly. Nitrogen-fixing bacteria convert N2 to ammonia (NH3), which plants can absorb as ammonium and use to synthesize amino acids and nucleotides.

questions.question: CRISPR-Cas9 differs from traditional restriction enzymes in that CRISPR-Cas9:
options: Can only cut RNA, not DNA
Uses a guide RNA to precisely target any specific DNA sequence
questions.answer: Uses a guide RNA to precisely target any specific DNA sequence
questions.explanation: CRISPR-Cas9 is programmable -- by changing the guide RNA sequence, researchers can target any DNA sequence in any organism. Restriction enzymes recognize fixed palindromic sequences and cannot be reprogrammed.

questions.question: During translation, the A site of the ribosome:
options: Holds the growing polypeptide chain on a tRNA
Is the site where the new aminoacyl-tRNA enters
Is where the peptide bond is formed between amino acids
Is where the uncharged tRNA exits the ribosome
questions.answer: Is the site where the new aminoacyl-tRNA enters
questions.explanation: The ribosome has three tRNA binding sites: A (aminoacyl) site where new aminoacyl-tRNAs enter, P (peptidyl) site where the growing polypeptide is held, and E (exit) site where uncharged tRNAs leave.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which observation by Darwin most directly supports the concept of natural selection" is: All species are perfectly adapted to their environment.
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Individuals within a species vary in heritable traits, and some variants survive and reproduce better.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why logistic growth produces an S-shaped curve?
options: Resources are unlimited in the environment
As population grows, competition and limiting factors slow the growth rate
The population reaches a maximum size and then goes extinct
questions.answer: As population grows, competition and limiting factors slow the growth rate
questions.explanation: In logistic growth, the per-capita growth rate decreases as population density increases because resources become limited. Growth approaches zero as N approaches K (carrying capacity), producing the sigmoid S-shaped curve.

questions.question: Primary succession is most likely to occur:
options: In a forest clearing after a wildfire
On bare volcanic rock following a lava flow
After a flood deposits new sediment over existing vegetation
questions.answer: On bare volcanic rock following a lava flow
questions.explanation: Primary succession occurs on bare substrate with no soil or biological legacy -- like new volcanic rock or bare rock exposed by glacial retreat. Pioneer species (lichens, mosses) colonize first, gradually building soil.

questions.question: A mutation changes the codon CAG (glutamine) to UAG. This type of mutation is best classified as a:
questions.answer: Nonsense mutation
questions.explanation: UAG is a stop codon. A mutation that changes a sense codon (coding for an amino acid) to a stop codon is a nonsense mutation. This causes premature termination of translation, producing a truncated nonfunctional protein.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "The Bottleneck Effect is a type of genetic drift that occurs when:" is: A population is drastically reduced in size by a catastrophic event.
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. A population is drastically reduced in size by a catastrophic event is correct.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the role of decomposers in the ecosystem?
options: They convert light energy into chemical energy
They break down dead organic matter and return nutrients to the soil
They transfer energy from producers to consumers
They fix atmospheric nitrogen into ammonia
questions.answer: They break down dead organic matter and return nutrients to the soil
questions.explanation: Decomposers (bacteria and fungi) break down dead organisms and organic waste, releasing nutrients (nitrogen, phosphorus, carbon) back into the soil and atmosphere where they can be used again by producers.

questions.question: What is the biological species concept?
options: A species is defined by its physical appearance and morphology
A species is a group of organisms that actually or potentially interbreed and produce fertile offspring
A species is defined by its genetic sequence similarity above 95%
A species is any group of organisms that lives in the same geographic area
questions.answer: A species is a group of organisms that actually or potentially interbreed and produce fertile offspring
questions.explanation: The biological species concept (Mayr, 1942) defines a species as a group of organisms that interbreed under natural conditions and produce viable, fertile offspring, emphasizing reproductive isolation as the defining criterion.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship where a clownfish gains shelter in a sea anemone while the anemone is unaffected?
questions.answer: Commensalism
questions.explanation: Commensalism is a +/0 interaction where one species benefits and the other is neither helped nor harmed. If the anemone is truly unaffected while the clownfish benefits from shelter, this is commensalism.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "During meiosis, genetic diversity is increased by:" is: DNA replication errors only.
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Crossing over (recombination) during prophase I and independent assortment of homologs.

questions.question: Human activities most directly contribute to increasing atmospheric CO2 by:
options: Combustion of fossil fuels and deforestation
Increased ocean evaporation due to warming
questions.answer: Combustion of fossil fuels and deforestation
questions.explanation: Burning fossil fuels releases carbon stored for millions of years as CO2. Deforestation removes carbon sinks (forests absorb CO2). Together, these rapidly increase atmospheric CO2, driving climate change.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why enzyme activity decreases at very high temperatures?
options: High temperature increases the activation energy of reactions
The enzyme is denatured -- its 3D structure unfolds, destroying the active site
Substrate molecules move too slowly to reach the enzyme
High temperatures cause the enzyme to leave the cell
questions.answer: The enzyme is denatured -- its 3D structure unfolds, destroying the active site
questions.explanation: Enzymes are proteins whose function depends on their precise 3D shape (especially the active site). At high temperatures, thermal energy disrupts hydrogen bonds and other non-covalent interactions, denaturing the enzyme.

questions.question: The founder effect is a type of genetic drift that occurs when:
options: A population grows rapidly from a small initial size after a disaster
A few individuals from a large population colonize a new isolated area
Natural selection acts rapidly on a small population
Multiple populations merge to form one larger population
questions.answer: A few individuals from a large population colonize a new isolated area
questions.explanation: The founder effect occurs when a small number of individuals from a larger population establish a new colony in an isolated area. The founders carry only a subset of the original genetic variation, reducing diversity.
